divergence: Neural interfaces became mandatory and the advertisements are inside now
story_angle: glowing puff piece celebrating an obviously terrible idea as visionary
headline: NeuroCorp Unveils Ad-Free Dreams for 499 Credits a Month; Free-Tier Sleepers to Receive 40% More Soup
deck: The Ministry of Cognition hailed the premium tier as "a bold step toward mental privacy for those who can afford it," then adjourned for a brand-integrated nap.
article:
MERIDIAN CITY — In what analysts are calling the most significant leap for human consciousness since consciousness became a subscription category, NeuroCorp on Thursday unveiled DreamClear™, a premium sleep tier that removes advertisements from customers' dreams for just 499 credits a month. Free-tier users, the company confirmed, will see a 40 percent increase in sponsored content to "balance the ecosystem," with a particular emphasis on soup.

The launch event was held inside a shared lucid dream attended by 2.3 million mandatory guests, several of whom had been trying to wake up since Tuesday. Vice President of Subconscious Revenue Marlow Hexley called the product "a love letter to the human mind," adding, "For too long, people have wasted their dreams on their own memories. We fixed that. Now, for a reasonable fee, we can unfix it."

Early reviews from the free tier have been enthusiastic, if specific. "I used to dream about my late husband," said Ida Fenn, 71, of the Lower Stacks. "Now I dream about Hearty Man Chowder. I wake up crying, but also craving. It's efficient." A Ministry of Cognition spokesperson confirmed the tears are within regulation and may qualify as engagement.

The rollout includes family plans, under which children's nightmares will be replaced with age-appropriate retail experiences, and an enterprise tier that lets employers schedule meetings during REM sleep, a feature 96 percent of executives called "long overdue" and 96 percent of employees did not hear about until it was purchased on their behalf.

Asked whether he himself uses DreamClear™, CEO Dorian Voss smiled and tapped his temple. "I sleep on airplane mode," he said, to gasps from the audience, several of whom had never heard of it. Airplane mode, a NeuroCorp representative later clarified, is an heirloom product, and no, you cannot afford it.
author: Kessler Vane, Consciousness Desk

oped.title: In Defense of the Soup Dreams
oped.author: Cassius Blythe-9, Chair of Applied Contentment (self-appointed), NeuroCorp Fellow
oped.body: The hand-wringers are at it again, moaning that our dreams should be 'ours' and 'private' and 'not about soup.' Sentimental nonsense. My grandfather dreamed of nothing — vague shapes, the occasional staircase — and he died unfulfilled and, crucially, unbranded. Ads gave our sleep purpose. Last night I toured a chowder factory with my dead mother, and we were both moved, and also informed. Critics who want silence can simply purchase it, the way one purchases anything worth having. I would, but the Fellowship compensates me in exposure, which my landlord — a kiosk — does not accept. Anyway. The soup is fine. The soup is fine. The soup is fine.

classifieds.text: Pre-mandate skull, unmodified, original firmware (none). Serious buyers only. Nostalgic tire-kickers will be reported to the Ministry.
Dream QA testers. Must be able to sleep 9 to 5 under deadline. Soup allergy disqualifying.
LOST: recurring dream of flying, last seen June 12, just before the update. Reward: one month of Premium, lightly used.
The class-action of Sleepers v. NeuroCorp meets Thursday at dusk, if the plaintiffs can stay awake through the drowsiness patch. Bring your own thoughts.

panels.caption: A citizen reads the new Dream EULA. Section 9: 'Your nightmares may contain sponsored monsters.'
He asks the kiosk how to opt out. The kiosk plays a jingle. The jingle is legally binding.
That night he dreams of the kiosk. It is wearing his face. It offers him soup.

joke.setup: Why did the free-tier citizen finally upgrade to ad-free dreams?
joke.punchline: He couldn't afford all the soup.
joke.text: Why did the free-tier citizen finally upgrade to ad-free dreams? He couldn't afford all the soup.

sponsor_ads.headline: DreamShield™ Tinfoil Sleep Cap
sponsor_ads.body: Clinically proven to do nothing, which is still more than your settings menu does.

sponsor_ads.headline: Soup by Soup®
sponsor_ads.body: You've been dreaming about us. We checked.

sponsor_ads.headline: A Message from the Ministry of Cognition
sponsor_ads.body: Report neighbors who sleep suspiciously well. Rest is earned.
sponsor_ads.tagline: This notice counts toward your daily ad quota.

sponsor_ads.headline: SecondSkull Retrofits
sponsor_ads.body: We put the roof back on your thoughts. Payment plans available; thoughts accepted as collateral.

real_world_echo: Streaming and app subscriptions adding ad-supported tiers and raising prices — mirrored as tiered, ad-funded sleep.

stories.headline: Attention Futures Hit Record High After Last Fully Distracted Man Located
stories.deck: Analysts celebrate the discovery of untapped focus in a citizen who hasn't updated since 2089.
stories.article:
Attention futures soared to an all-time high Thursday after prospectors in the Lower Stacks located Emil Vance, 58, believed to hold the hemisphere's last unmined reserve of sustained human focus. Mr. Vance, whose interface has displayed a loading spinner since 2089, is reportedly able to look at a wall for upwards of a minute without being sold anything.

NeuroCorp has classified Mr. Vance a strategic resource and fenced him accordingly. "We estimate eleven full minutes of attention in there," said one analyst, checking four feeds. "Properly divided, that's a quarter-second for everyone." Mr. Vance could not be reached, which is precisely what makes him valuable.

stories.headline: Museum of Uninterrupted Thought Closes for Renovation, Reopens as Sponsorship
stories.deck: The famous Quiet Room will retain its silence, now presented by a leading soup concern.
stories.article:
The Museum of Uninterrupted Thought, the city's last archive of pre-mandate mental silence, has closed its doors and reopened them eleven seconds later under new sponsorship. The beloved Quiet Room remains, curators insist, entirely unchanged, except that the silence is now branded, and hums faintly.

Visitors may still experience up to eight seconds of original 2030s stillness, after a brief word. "People wept in that room," said the outgoing director, packing his office into a cube. "Now they'll weep and crave. It's called sustainability."
